children.name: Tiliqua
children.description: Blue-tongued skinks of Australia.

children.name: Tiliqua scincoides
children.description: Flashes vivid cobalt-blue tongue as a threat display contrasting with the pink mouth; gives birth to live young; widely kept as a pet; ground-dwelling in open woodland.

children.name: Tiliqua gigas
children.description: Similar to Eastern; New Guinea and Maluku Islands.

children.name: Tiliqua rugosa
children.description: Stout, slow-moving skink with rough, pine-cone-like scales and a stumpy tail that mimics the head; forms long-term monogamous pair bonds unique among lizards. Also called bobtail or pinecone lizard.

children.name: Tiliqua adelaidensis
children.description: Presumed extinct for decades until rediscovered in 1992 living exclusively inside spider burrows in native grasslands north of Adelaide. Critically dependent on trapdoor spider burrows for thermoregulation and protection.

children.name: Tiliqua multifasciata
children.description: Inhabits arid interior Australia; banded pattern distinguishes it from the eastern species. Viviparous like all blue-tongues, producing small litters in late summer.

children.name: Tiliqua nigrolutea
children.description: Found in cool montane and coastal heathland of south-eastern Australia and Tasmania; distinctive cream and dark blotched pattern. Tolerates lower temperatures than most skinks and is active on mild overcast days.

children.name: Plestiodon
children.description: North American skinks.

children.name: Plestiodon fasciatus
children.description: Juveniles have iridescent electric-blue tails to draw predator attention; common under logs in eastern forests; tail autotomy standard defence.

children.name: Plestiodon laticeps
children.description: Males develop bright orange-red heads in breeding season; arboreal.

children.name: Plestiodon obsoletus
children.description: Largest North American skink, reaching 35 cm; inhabits rocky hillsides and grasslands of the Great Plains and Mexican plateau. Female guards egg clutch until hatching, unusual maternal behaviour for a lizard.

children.name: Plestiodon inexpectatus
children.description: Nearly identical to P. fasciatus but distinguishable by scale counts; confined to southeastern coastal plain. Often found in pine flatwoods and under rotting logs near moisture.

children.name: Plestiodon skiltonianus
children.description: Slender species of western North America from British Columbia to Baja California; frequents rocky chaparral and mixed forest edges. Named after Darius Nash Skilton, an American naturalist who collected specimens in Oregon.

children.name: Scincus
children.description: Desert sand-swimming skinks.

children.name: Scincus scincus
children.description: Swims through loose desert sand using sinusoidal body movements; polished overlapping scales minimise friction; can dive below the surface in under a second to escape heat or predators.

children.name: Scincus mitranus
children.description: Restricted to the sand deserts of the Arabian Peninsula and adjacent Iran; slightly more robust than S. scincus with a broader snout adapted for sand-diving. Active mainly at dawn and dusk to avoid extreme midday heat.

children.name: Eutropis
children.description: Sun skinks of Asia.

children.name: Eutropis multifasciata
children.description: Extremely abundant in SE Asian gardens and farmland; active basker on rocks and logs.

children.name: Eutropis macularia
children.description: Widely distributed from Sri Lanka and India across Southeast Asia; metallic bronze flanks with scattered dark spots give it its common name. A diurnal insectivore of open grassland, gardens, and forest clearings.

children.name: Eutropis longicaudatus
children.description: One of the larger Asian sun skinks, with a tail roughly twice the body length; inhabits secondary forest and scrub in Indochina and Thailand. Active forager of invertebrates in leaf litter.

children.name: Mabuya
children.description: New World skinks.

children.name: Mabuya mabouya
children.description: One of the few reptiles with true placental reproduction; embryos receive all nutrition via placenta as in mammals.

children.name: Mabuya heathi
children.description: Endemic to the caatinga and semi-arid scrub of northeastern Brazil; like other Mabuya it is viviparous with a highly developed chorioallantoic placenta. Named after Harold Heath, American biologist.

children.name: Trachylepis
children.description: African sun skinks.

children.name: Trachylepis striata
children.description: Common in sub-Saharan Africa; rock-dwelling; speed main defence.

children.name: Trachylepis quinquetaeniata
children.description: Bright five-striped juvenile coloration fades with age; widespread from Egypt through East Africa and into Arabia. Commonly seen basking on stone walls and termite mounds.

children.name: Trachylepis varia
children.description: Extremely variable in pattern across its wide sub-Saharan range; often found on rocky outcrops and kopjes in grassland. One of the most ecologically flexible skinks on the continent.

children.name: Trachylepis spilogaster
children.description: Inhabits dry savanna and semi-desert from Sudan and Ethiopia through the Horn of Africa; slender body with spotted underside. Active forager that retreats quickly into rock crevices when threatened.

children.name: Ctenotus
children.description: Striped skinks, the most species-rich lizard genus in Australia.

children.name: Ctenotus taeniolatus
children.description: One of the most familiar Australian skinks; boldly striped with a reddish-copper tail; abundant in coastal heath and woodland. A swift diurnal forager of insects and small invertebrates.

children.name: Ctenotus robustus
children.description: Largest and most powerfully built of the Ctenotus skinks; widespread across eastern and central Australia in a variety of dry habitats. Feeds on larger arthropods and occasionally small lizards.

children.name: Ctenotus pantherinus
children.description: Striking species with a complex pattern of spots, ocelli, and stripes across the back; found in arid interior Australia in spinifex grassland. One of the more ornate members of its genus.

children.name: Ctenotus regius
children.description: A mid-sized Ctenotus of the arid and semi-arid zones with clean pale stripes on a dark dorsum; typically found in sandy soils with spinifex. Fast and wary, retreating into grass tussocks when disturbed.

children.name: Lerista
children.description: Sand-swimmers with reduced limbs, illustrating limb loss in action.

children.name: Lerista bougainvillii
children.description: Named after French navigator Louis Antoine de Bougainville; one of the larger Lerista with reduced but functional limbs. Occupies sandy soils in coastal and sub-coastal southern Australia.

children.name: Lerista punctatovittata
children.description: Slender burrowing skink of arid South Australia and Western Australia with vestigial hindlimbs; dorsum bears rows of small dark dots. Spends most of its time beneath leaf litter or in loose sandy soil.

children.name: Cryptoblepharus
children.description: Snake-eyed skinks with transparent fixed eyelids, found on rocky coasts and islands.

children.name: Cryptoblepharus boutonii
children.description: A remarkably widespread species found on rocky shores and coral islands from East Africa to the Pacific; fused, transparent lower eyelid gives the eye a snake-like appearance. Highly salt-tolerant and often seen foraging in the intertidal zone.

children.name: Cryptoblepharus virgatus
children.description: Common on rock faces and tree trunks in eastern Australia; adhesive toe pads allow it to hunt on vertical surfaces. One of Australia's most frequently observed skinks in coastal areas.

children.name: Cryptoblepharus leschenaultii
children.description: Named after Jean-Baptiste Leschenault de la Tour, French botanist on Baudin's expedition; found from India through Southeast Asia to northern Australia. Lives on the trunks of large trees and rocky outcrops.

children.name: Emoia
children.description: Pacific skinks highly diverse across Melanesia and Oceania.

children.name: Emoia cyanura
children.description: Brilliant sky-blue tail contrasts with the bronze-brown body; abundant in coconut plantations and disturbed coastal habitats across the Pacific. A highly successful coloniser of oceanic islands.

children.name: Emoia atrocostata
children.description: Specialised for life on rocky coasts and intertidal boulders from the Philippines to the Solomon Islands; dark coloration absorbs heat rapidly in shaded coastal zones. Can withstand brief wave immersion.

children.name: Emoia caeruleocauda
children.description: One of the most widespread Emoia across Pacific island groups from New Guinea to Samoa and Fiji; vivid blue tail particularly bright in juveniles. Adapts readily to gardens and village environments.

children.name: Carlia
children.description: Rainbow skinks of Australia and New Guinea with iridescent colouration.

children.name: Carlia tetradactyla
children.description: Slender, fast-moving skink of coastal heathland in Queensland and New South Wales; males develop vivid orange flanks in breeding season. Named for having four toes on each foot.

children.name: Carlia fusca
children.description: Widespread in northern Australia and New Guinea in wet tropical and monsoon forest edges; dark dorsal flecks over a brown background. An agile insectivore most active in morning sun.

children.name: Sphenomorphus
children.description: Forest skinks of Asia and Australasia, among the most species-rich skink genera.

children.name: Sphenomorphus indicus
children.description: Common in leaf litter of Indian and Southeast Asian forests; secretive burrower that hunts small invertebrates under decaying logs. Smooth brown scales provide excellent camouflage among forest debris.

children.name: Sphenomorphus scutatus
children.description: Robust forest skink of montane forests in Borneo and the Philippines; enlarged shield-like dorsal scales give it its name. A slow-moving, largely fossorial species found under bark and logs.

children.name: Lygosoma
children.description: Cylindrical burrowing skinks of Africa and Asia; gave name to the old subfamily Lygosominae.

children.name: Lygosoma punctata
children.description: Worm-like in proportions; smooth, highly polished scales enable rapid movement through damp soil and leaf litter across South and Southeast Asia. Largely fossorial, emerging at dusk to hunt small invertebrates.

children.name: Oligosoma
children.description: New Zealand skinks, the dominant lizard genus of Aotearoa.

children.name: Oligosoma grande
children.description: New Zealand's largest skink, up to 27 cm; endemic to rocky schist outcrops of Otago; classified Endangered due to predation by introduced stoats, rats, and cats. Forms stable social groups with pair bonds lasting several years.

children.name: Oligosoma otagense
children.description: Co-occurs with the Grand Skink on schist rock outcrops in Central Otago, New Zealand; bold black-and-yellow pattern on a greenish background. Critically threatened by mammalian predators introduced to New Zealand.

children.name: Oligosoma infrapunctatum
children.description: Widespread across both main islands of New Zealand in grassland, scrub, and coastal habitats; underside spotted with pale flecks. One of the more common mainland skinks of Aotearoa.

children.name: Oligosoma nigriplantare
children.description: Widespread on coastal rocks and sandy shores throughout New Zealand; sole of foot is black, giving the species name. Often found sheltering under driftwood above the tideline.

children.name: Niveoscincus
children.description: Snow skinks of Tasmania and the Australian Alps, cold-climate specialists.

children.name: Niveoscincus ocellatus
children.description: Most widespread and common of the Tasmanian snow skinks; dorsal pattern of pale-edged dark spots (ocelli) makes it easy to identify. Found from sea level to alpine heath, tolerating snow for weeks.

children.name: Niveoscincus metallicus
children.description: Iridescent coppery-bronze scales reflect sunlight; found in cool temperate forests and alpine areas of Tasmania and the Bass Strait islands. Basks extensively on rocks to maintain body temperature in cool climates.

children.name: Corucia
children.description: Monotypic genus; the largest living skink in the world.

children.name: Corucia zebrata
children.description: The world's largest skink at up to 76 cm; endemic to the Solomon Islands; prehensile tail used to grip branches like a fifth limb. Strictly herbivorous and arboreal, living in family groups with extended parental care rare in reptiles.

children.name: Egernia
children.description: Australian social skinks renowned for complex family structures.

children.name: Egernia cunninghami
children.description: Large, spiny-scaled skink named after Allan Cunningham, British botanist who collected in Australia. Lives in stable family groups in rocky crevices on granite outcrops; viviparous with strong social bonds between adults and offspring.

children.name: Egernia stokesii
children.description: Named after John Lort Stokes, British naval officer who charted Australian coasts; inhabits arid mulga and gidgee scrubland. Shelters in communal rock crevices with spiny tail used to block the entrance to the burrow.

children.name: Egernia whitii
children.description: Named after John White, surgeon-general on the First Fleet who described Australian fauna; widespread in rocky habitats of south-eastern Australia. Lives in stable multi-generational family units, one of the best-studied examples of sociality in lizards.

children.name: Tribolonotus
children.description: Crocodile skinks of New Guinea and the Solomon Islands, armoured with keeled scales.

children.name: Tribolonotus gracilis
children.description: Distinctive bright orange-red eye ring and four rows of keeled, spine-like scales along the back give it a prehistoric appearance; endemic to New Guinea. Emits a loud squeak when handled and will play dead, unusual defensive behaviours for a lizard.

children.name: Tribolonotus novaeguineae
children.description: Inhabits humid tropical forest floor of New Guinea; armoured dorsal scales arranged in neat rows recalling crocodilian osteoderms. Largely nocturnal and secretive under fallen logs near streams.

children.name: Prasinohaema
children.description: Green-blooded skinks of New Guinea with biliverdin-saturated blood.

children.name: Prasinohaema virens
children.description: The most studied of the green-blooded skinks; blood, muscles, bones, and tongue are all vivid lime-green due to extraordinary concentrations of biliverdin that would be toxic in most vertebrates. Found in montane forests of New Guinea; the adaptive benefit of the green blood is still debated.

children.name: Prasinohaema prehensicauda
children.description: Prehensile tail assists climbing in the understorey of New Guinean montane rainforest; shares the green blood pigmentation of all Prasinohaema. Arboreal habits reduce competition with ground-dwelling relatives.

children.name: Eumeces
children.description: Old World skinks, formerly a broad catch-all genus now restricted to Asia and North Africa.

children.name: Eumeces schneideri
children.description: One of the largest Old World skinks, reaching 40 cm; vivid orange lateral spots brighten during breeding season; found in North Africa and the Middle East to Central Asia. Partially fossorial and omnivorous, eating plant matter as well as insects.

children.name: Eumeces taeniolatus
children.description: Slender Asian species with pale dorsal stripes on a dark brown background; distributed from Iran through Pakistan and Afghanistan into India. Inhabits rocky semi-desert and scrub where it shelters under flat stones.

children.name: Lipinia
children.description: Small forest-floor skinks of Southeast Asia and Pacific islands.

children.name: Lipinia noctua
children.description: Tiny yellow-striped skink found across a vast arc of Pacific islands from the Marianas to Fiji; among the most successfully distributed small lizards in Oceania. Often associated with coconut husks and forest edge habitats.

children.name: Lankascincus
children.description: Sri Lanka endemic skinks of forest and plantation habitats.

children.name: Lankascincus fallax
children.description: The most widespread and frequently encountered skink on the island of Sri Lanka; found from sea level to mid-elevation forests and coconut plantations. Slender and quick, foraging actively by day among leaf litter.

children.name: Lankascincus dorsalis
children.description: Endemic to Sri Lanka's wet zone forests; distinguished from its congeners by a prominent pale dorsal stripe running the length of the body. Threatened by deforestation of lowland rainforest.
